Bonus Challenge — Doppler Analysis
The absorption lines in this star's spectrum are shifted from their rest positions. Compare the observed spectrum (top) against the reference spectrum (bottom) and determine the star's motion.
Observed spectrum:
Laboratory reference (at rest):
Is this star moving toward us or away from us?
Bonus Challenge — Kepler's Third Law
Using T² = a³ / M — where T = orbital period (years), M = stellar mass (M☉), a = orbital distance (AU) — determine where this planet's orbit places it relative to the habitable zone.
Show your working — use the formula step by step:
Step 1  —  Square the period:
T²  =  ²  =  ?
Step 2  —  Multiply by stellar mass:
T² × M  =   ×  =  ?
Step 3  —  Cube root → your answer for a (AU):
a = ∛  =   AU
Where does this planet's calculated orbit place it?
Element Identification
Which element produces the highlighted line?
Stellar Evolution — Optional Bonus
Based on this star's mass and spectral class, predict its ultimate fate when it exhausts its nuclear fuel.
What will this star become?
Reference Library
Each element's unique emission signature — bright coloured lines on black, as seen in a laboratory discharge tube. These are the patterns you'll identify in the Element ID puzzle. Wavelengths shown in nanometres (nm).
💡 In Tier A puzzles the question shows one of these patterns and asks which element it is. Use this chart to memorise them.
Ground-state electron configurations for all elements in the game pool. Valence electrons — shown in highlighted — produce the emission lines you identify in puzzles.
How to write an electron configuration — and the Noble Gas shorthand

An electron configuration tells you how electrons are arranged in an atom's shells. The game asks you to write them using Noble Gas notation — here's how both work.

Step 1 — Fill the subshells in Aufbau order

Electrons fill orbitals from lowest energy to highest, following this fixed sequence:

1s → 2s → 2p → 3s → 3p → 4s → 3d → 4p → 5s → 4d → 5p → 6s → 4f → 5d → 6p …

Each subshell holds a maximum: s=2 · p=6 · d=10 · f=14

Step 2 — Example: Iron (Fe, #26)

Fill 26 electrons in order:

1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s² 3d⁶

Check: 2+2+6+2+6+2+6 = 26 ✓

Step 3 — Shorten it with Noble Gas notation

Find the noble gas whose electron count is just below your element's. Replace those electrons with its symbol in [brackets].

Iron has 26 electrons. The nearest noble gas below 26 is Argon [Ar] = 18 electrons.

1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s² 3d⁶
[Ar] 4s² 3d⁶

Everything after [Ar] is the valence shell — the electrons that create spectral lines.

The six noble gas cores
[He] = 1s² (2e)
[Ne] = …2p⁶ (10e)
[Ar] = …3p⁶ (18e)
[Kr] = …4p⁶ (36e)
[Xe] = …5p⁶ (54e)
[Rn] = …6p⁶ (86e)
💡 Tip: Click any gold-bordered element on the Periodic Table tab to see its full Aufbau walkthrough step by step — watching it built is the fastest way to learn the pattern.
Gold-bordered = in game pool · Click any element for a worked example · Start with H if you're new → For notation help, see the ⚛️ Configurations tab

"We seek not to own the stars, but to understand them."
Mission

The Fifth Sun Initiative was founded after historians and astrophysicists noticed that ancient civilisations — separated by thousands of years and thousands of miles — always asked the same questions about the stars.

Auto-cycles through each tool with a brief overview. Your crew guides you as you play.

Reading the Light

Every star sends us a message. It's written in missing colours.

When starlight spreads into a rainbow, certain colours are absorbed by elements in the star's outer layers. Those missing colours appear as dark gaps — absorption lines. The pattern is a fingerprint: hydrogen always absorbs at the same wavelengths, calcium at the same wavelengths.

Think barcode. Match the barcode → identify the star.
Hydrogen: 656nm (red), 486nm (blue-green), 434nm (violet). Calcium: 393nm and 397nm.

Three things to look for

  • How many lines — hot stars have few. Cool stars have so many the spectrum looks nearly black.
  • Where they cluster — heavy on the left (blue) = hot. Spread through the right (red) = cool.
  • Spacing — evenly spaced bold lines = A-type hydrogen. Tight dense clusters = cooler metal-rich stars.

Emission spectra are the inverse — bright lines on black. The positions are identical. Match the positions, not the colour.

Spectral reference card — all seven classes
Class
Temperature
Spectrum preview
Key lines
Hab. index
Scale: 380nm (violet, left) → 700nm (red, right).

Seven Kinds of Star

Oh Be A Fine Girl/Guy, Kiss Me — the mnemonic astronomers have used for over a century.

Stars are sorted hottest (O) to coolest (M) by surface temperature. In Act III, use temperature first to narrow to 1–2 classes, then confirm with the spectral description.

In Act III you already know temperature, luminosity, and radius — classification should feel like confirmation, not guesswork.
O
30,000–50,000 K · Blue-white · Very few, widely-spaced lines. Hottest and rarest. Lethal UV output. Lives only a few million years before exploding.
B
10,000–30,000 K · Blue-white · Few lines, some helium. Still extremely hot. Rigel and Regulus are B stars.
A
7,500–10,000 K · White · Bold, evenly-spaced hydrogen lines. Easiest to spot — Balmer series is strongest here. Sirius and Vega are A stars.
F
6,000–7,500 K · Yellow-white · Hydrogen weakens, calcium and iron appear. Procyon and Polaris are F stars.
G
5,200–6,000 K · Yellow · Rich, dense spectrum across the middle. Our Sun's class. Long-lived and stable. Tau Ceti is a G star.
K
3,700–5,200 K · Orange · Very dense, molecular bands beginning. Lives even longer than G stars. Epsilon Eridani is a K star.
M
2,400–3,700 K · Red · Extremely dense — nearly black in the red half. Most common star in the galaxy. TiO bands are the key identifier. Proxima Centauri is an M star.
Classification buttons don't show temperature ranges — use the data above. Temperature first, spectral description confirms.

Could Something Live There?

Four instruments. Four readings. One question.

Act II scans the candidate planet. Read the numbers, pick your verdict — labels are hidden until you submit. Trust the numbers.

Quick reference
Pressure
0.3 – 3.0 atm ✓
Temperature
−20°C to +70°C ✓
Magnetic field
≥ 8 μT ✓
Orbital stability
≥ 55 / 100 ✓
4 in range = Viable  ·  2–3 in range = Marginal  ·  0–1 in range = Hostile
That's all you need. The science behind each threshold is below.
Why these thresholds? (the real science)

Pressure (0.3–3.0 atm) — Earth is 1.0 atm. Too thin and water evaporates. Too thick and you get a crushing greenhouse. Mars is 0.006 atm — far too low.

Temperature (−20 to +70°C) — liquid water range with a margin for extremophiles. Some microbes survive at −20°C in brine and up to +120°C in hydrothermal vents.

Magnetic field (≥ 8 μT) — without it, stellar wind strips the atmosphere over geological time. Mars lost its field ~4 billion years ago and its atmosphere soon after.

Orbital stability (≥ 55) — a highly elliptical orbit causes extreme seasonal swings. A planet swinging from boiling to frozen can't maintain liquid water long enough for life.

Going Deeper

Every bonus puzzle is optional. Extra points if you try. No penalty if you skip — except the ones marked ★ which are part of the main campaign.

What unlocks and when
◈ Doppler — is this star moving? +75 pts · Tier 2+
✦ Element ID — which element made this pattern? +125–500 pts · 10+ stars found
⬡ Kepler — is the orbit habitable? +100 pts · habitable worlds
🚫 Forbidden Lines — nebula vs star spectra, +150 pts · Tier 3+
⚡ Anomaly ★ — identify an object that breaks all the rules. +200 pts · every 8–12 stars
🚫 Forbidden Lines — nebula vs star spectra. +150 pts · Tier 3+
📡 First Contact ★ — decode a 5-part signal from a habitable world. +150–300 pts per act
⚛️ Config Sequence — order the electrons correctly. +100 pts · after element ID
◈ Doppler shift — is it moving?

When a star moves toward us its light compresses — spectral lines shift left (bluer). Moving away stretches them right (redder). Find the same line in both strips and check which direction it moved.

Easiest line: H-alpha at 656nm — rightmost prominent line. Left of reference = approaching. Right = receding.
⬡ Kepler's Third Law — where does this planet orbit?

Formula: a = ∛(T² × M) — T = orbital period in years, M = stellar mass in solar masses, a = distance in AU.

Example: T=1yr, M=1 → 1² × 1 = 1 → ∛1 = 1.0 AU — Earth's orbit. ✓
✦ Element ID — read the emission fingerprint

Each element emits at specific wavelengths — its fingerprint. Phase 1 (0–4 correct): four reference strips to compare. Phase 2 (5–14 correct): name-only choices — no visual help. Phase 3 (15+ correct): free-type — name, symbol, or atomic number all accepted. The ⊞ Block Table and 🔬 Spectra Reference buttons provide lifelines throughout.

After a correct ID, a follow-up Config Sequence asks you to arrange electron subshells in the right order — the same electrons that produced the spectral lines you just matched.

🌟 Stellar evolution — what will this star become?

Mass decides everything. A random jitter is added to each star's mass — the answer isn't always white dwarf.

  • Below 0.5 M☉ → Black Dwarf
  • 0.5 – 8 M☉ → White Dwarf (our Sun's fate)
  • 8 – 20 M☉ → Neutron Star
  • Above 20 M☉ → Black Hole
⚡ The Anomaly — when the rules break down ★

Every 8–12 correctly classified stars, something arrives that doesn't fit OBAFGKM at all. A badge appears: ANOMALOUS SIGNAL. Four choices, one correct. +200 pts.

Possible anomalies include: Magnetar (X-ray output 10⁸× too high), Tidal Disruption Event (brightening on a weekly timescale), Pulsar (sub-second radio pulses more precise than atomic clocks), Quasar (100× brighter than the entire Milky Way, 3 billion light-years away).

Anomalies are integrated into the Hull Integrity system — wrong answers cost a hull segment.
🚫 Forbidden Lines — nebula vs star spectra

In deep space, gas clouds emit spectral lines that are physically impossible to reproduce in an Earth laboratory — because they require pressures so low they can't be created here. These are called forbidden lines.

The puzzle shows two spectra side by side: one stellar, one nebular. You identify which lines are forbidden and what the pressure difference tells you about the environment. Unlocks in Tier 3+ sectors and feeds into the First Contact signal decode.

📡 First Contact — a five-act signal ★

Every time you correctly identify a habitable world and establish a colony, you decode more of a signal arriving from the direction of that star. Five acts, each a different type of interactive puzzle:

  1. Act I — Is this signal natural or artificial? Identify the mathematical sequence. (20% decoded)
  2. Act II — A binary image of their solar system. How many planets? Which one do they live on? (40%)
  3. Act III — They've sent their star's spectrum. Use your classification skills to identify it. (60%)
  4. Act IV — The timestamp. Calculate when the signal was sent using the distance and light travel time. (80%)
  5. Act V — The final transmission. No puzzle — just the revelation. (100%)
The science you learn in this game is not just useful for studying stars. It turns out to be the universal language — the one thing any spacefaring civilisation would have in common with us.
🛡 Hull Integrity — the mission failure condition

Five green hull segments appear in the HUD. Each wrong answer damages one segment. If all five are lost, the current sector resets — but all previous sectors, score, and credits are preserved. Only the sector you are currently in resets.

Repair: every 5 consecutive correct answers restores one segment.

The hull resets to full when you enter a new sector. It only protects the work you have already done — you can never lose more than the current sector.
🔮 Special events — variable stars, flares, binaries

Every 6–9 scans, a special event star appears. The classification puzzle is identical — but the badge adds science context and a bonus:

  • 🔮 Variable Star — the spectrum pulses in brightness. After correct classification, use the pulsation period to calculate the star's distance via the Period-Luminosity relation. +75 pts bonus.
  • ☀️ Stellar Flare — after classification, assess the UV impact on any orbiting planets. +50 pts bonus.
  • ⚡ Spectroscopic Binary — after classification, derive the mass ratio of both stars from the velocity amplitude. +60 pts bonus.

How to Play

The whole game in four steps:
1. Click a star → hit SCAN → match its spectrum to identify the stellar class.
2. If it looks habitable, survey the planet — four instruments, one verdict.
3. Classify the star. Establish colonies on habitable worlds.
4. Watch for Anomalies, decode the First Contact signal, and keep your Hull Integrity above zero.

Controls

  • Click a star to target it → SCAN to begin analysis
  • Drag the map to pan · Minimap bottom-right shows your position
  • ? reopens this guide · 📖 opens the science glossary · A cycles font size (small / medium / large)
  • ♪ Music toggles audio · the volume slider adjusts level · track label shows Cosmos or Battle depending on sector tier
  • ⬡ Sector Jump unlocks after your first colony · includes the ⚡ Survey Sprint timed challenge
  • Credits (cr) — earned alongside score points. Spend them on hints during Element ID puzzles. Each hint costs more than the last (50 → 100 → 200 cr).
🛡 Hull Integrity
The 5 green segments in the HUD are your Hull Integrity. Each wrong answer costs one segment.
Lose all five and the current sector resets — your score and previous sectors stay safe.
Repair: 5 correct answers in a row restores one segment.
Advanced operations (appear after correct emission match)
  • Doppler (Tier 2+) — compare line positions to find if the star is approaching or receding. +75 pts.
  • Element ID (optional → mandatory at 20 stars) — identify an element from its emission spectrum. +125 to +500 pts.
  • Kepler (habitable worlds) — calculate orbital distance using T²=a³/M. +100 pts.
  • Forbidden Lines (Tier 3+) — compare stellar and nebular spectra, identify why certain lines are impossible in a lab. +150 pts.
Sector difficulty & spectrum types
  • Tier 1 — absorption and emission spectra. Home Nebula, Habitable Belt.
  • Tier 2 — more emission spectra + Doppler puzzles. White Ring, Red Dwarf Field.
  • Tier 3 — binary composite spectra dominate. Inner Rim, Deep Field.
  • Tier 4 — all of the above + formula-based classification. Unlocks after visiting all 6 base sectors.
Scoring reference
Emission match+100 + streak (+20/streak)
First discovery+50
Planetary survey+75 + streak
Classification+125 + streak
Doppler+75
Element ID — Tier A+125
Element ID — Tier B+250 · wrong −10
Element ID — Tier C+500 · wrong −10
Kepler bonus+100
Stellar evolution+150
Colony+125 to +200
Progression unlocks
  • Colony 1 — Sector Jump unlocked
  • Colony 3 — Telescope Deep Scan (50 cr/scan)
  • 10 discoveries — Element ID mandatory · Tier B active
  • 15+ habitable worlds — First Contact signal fully decoded · 5-act transmission
  • All 6 sectors visited — Tier IV sectors unlock

Research Mode

Research Mode unlocks after discovering your first star in The Heliopause. It adds two new puzzle variants to the main game loop — the same four acts, but Act III and the advanced operations become significantly more demanding.

Research Mode is a prestige layer — it doesn't replace the main game. All ten sectors remain playable with their standard puzzles. Research Mode variants appear when replaying sectors after the unlock.
HR Diagram Placement (replaces classification buttons)

Instead of clicking O B A F G K M, you place the star on an interactive Hertzsprung-Russell diagram with five clickable regions: Supergiant, Giant, Main Sequence, Subgiant, and White Dwarf.

The key insight this puzzle teaches: temperature alone doesn't classify a star. A G-type temperature (5,750K) could belong to a main sequence dwarf, a subgiant, a giant, or even a white dwarf. Luminosity and radius together with temperature determine where on the diagram a star belongs — and therefore its luminosity class (Ia, II, III, IV, V).

The diagram shows your target star as a yellow dot. Clicking a wrong region reveals why that region is ruled out by the data — part of the learning is understanding what each region of the HR diagram actually represents.
Spectroscopic Binary Mass (new mandatory puzzle for composite spectra)

When a composite binary spectrum is detected, both stars' absorption lines are visible shifting back and forth in opposite directions as they orbit. The velocity amplitude of each star is inversely proportional to its mass:

M₁ / M₂ = V₂ / V₁

The more massive star moves slower — it has a smaller orbit around the centre of mass. The puzzle shows a radial velocity graph with two sinusoidal curves (one per star) and gives you both amplitudes. You must identify which star is more massive and estimate the mass ratio.

This is exactly how stellar masses are measured in practice. Spica (α Virginis), one of the brightest stars in the sky, was first determined to be a binary system in 1890 using this technique.
Heliopause Advanced Operations (three new analyses — all optional)

Stars in the Heliopause sector come with three extra analysis techniques after classification. Each is optional (+150 pts each) but together they give you a complete stellar portrait.

1 — Lithium Depletion Age

Lithium (Li) is destroyed inside stars as they get older — the hotter the core, the faster it burns away. The spectrum shows two nearby lines: Li at 670.8nm and Fe at 670.3nm. Compare their depths:

  • Li much stronger than Fe → young star (under ~1 billion years)
  • Li and Fe roughly equal → middle-aged (~1–5 billion years)
  • Li much weaker than Fe (or absent) → old star (over 5 billion years)
The ratio changes slowly with age — you're making an estimate, not an exact measurement. Look at which line has a deeper dip in the spectrum.

2 — Radial Velocity Planet Detection

A planet orbiting a star makes the star wobble slightly toward and away from us. This wobble appears as a rhythmic Doppler shift — the spectral lines shift blue then red then blue again as the star moves. The canvas shows a velocity curve over time.

  • A smooth wave means one planet orbiting cleanly
  • A large amplitude (tall wave) means a massive planet
  • A short period (fast waves) means the planet is close in, hot
The question asks you to identify what the curve tells you — not to calculate anything. Read the pattern, trust your eyes.

3 — Interstellar Medium Absorption

Light from distant Heliopause stars passes through clouds of gas floating in interstellar space. That gas adds its own absorption lines on top of the star's lines. How to tell them apart:

  • Stellar lines — broad, fuzzy edges, caused by the hot turbulent stellar atmosphere
  • Interstellar lines — narrow, sharp, thin — like a knife cut in the spectrum

The puzzle highlights candidate lines and asks which are interstellar. Look for the sharp narrow ones. The two most common interstellar tracers are Ca II K at 393nm and Na I D at 589nm.

Research Log (replaces Mission Report)

In Research Mode, the session summary becomes a formatted Research Log showing all derived quantities alongside measured parameters. For each star analysed it records:

  • Spectral type and luminosity class (from HR diagram placement)
  • Binary mass ratio if a composite spectrum was detected
  • Lithium age estimate and Li/Fe line ratio (if Heliopause sector)
  • Orbital period and planet classification from radial velocity curve
  • ISM tracer lines identified (Ca II K, Na I D)
  • Electron configuration for each identified element

The Research Log can be printed or copied — it's formatted like a brief observational report suitable for submission as a lab exercise at introductory university level.

Curriculum alignment: The HR diagram placement puzzle maps to stellar evolution units in AP Physics, IB Physics HL, and introductory astrophysics courses. The spectroscopic binary mass puzzle corresponds to exercises in Carroll & Ostlie's Introduction to Modern Astrophysics (Chapter 7). The Research Log format mirrors standard observational astronomy lab reports.
Use the tabs above to navigate
Session Summary
Mission Report
Accuracy by puzzle type
Shareable report (copy for teacher / portfolio):
Advanced Analysis — Lithium Depletion
Lithium (Li) at 670.8nm is destroyed inside stars as they age. Compare the lithium line depth to the nearby iron line at 670.3nm. A strong lithium line means a young star; a weak or absent line means an old one.
💡 How to read this: Find the two dips near the right side of the spectrum — yellow marker = Li, green marker = Fe. Is Li deeper (stronger) or shallower (weaker) than Fe?
Estimate this star's age based on lithium line strength:
Advanced Analysis — Radial Velocity Planet Detection
An orbiting planet makes its host star wobble. This wobble Doppler-shifts the star's spectral lines periodically. The graph below shows how this star's radial velocity has changed over time. Read the pattern to detect the planet.
💡 How to read this: The wave moving up = star moving toward us (blueshift). Moving down = moving away (redshift). A repeating smooth wave = a planet is orbiting. A tall wave = massive planet. A fast wave = close-in orbit.
What can you conclude from this radial velocity curve?
Advanced Analysis — Interstellar Medium Absorption
This star's light passed through interstellar gas clouds before reaching us. The cloud added its own narrow absorption lines on top of the star's broader stellar lines. Interstellar lines are sharper and do not Doppler shift with the star.
Spectrum — stellar lines (broad) + interstellar lines (narrow):
💡 How to tell them apart: Interstellar lines = sharp narrow spikes. Stellar lines = broad fuzzy dips. The most common interstellar lines appear at Ca II K ~393nm (blue-violet) and Na I D ~589nm (yellow).
380nm450nm520nm590nm660nm700nm
Which of these wavelengths are interstellar absorption lines (not stellar)?

Academy Archives
Spectra Explorer — Academy Archives
Free · No accounts · No data collection · Single HTML file · Works in any modern browser · v1.0.0

What students learn

Spectra Explorer teaches by doing. Every concept is embedded in a puzzle students must solve — not a fact sheet they must memorise. The game adapts in difficulty automatically across 10 sectors.

1
Stellar spectral classification — identify star types O B A F G K M by reading absorption and emission line patterns in realistic spectra
2
Spectral line identification — distinguish absorption spectra (dark lines on rainbow) from emission spectra (bright lines on black); match patterns to candidates
3
Doppler effect — determine radial velocity direction (approaching/receding) from spectral line displacement relative to a reference
4
Orbital Navigation — apply T²=a³/M to calculate semi-major axis, then determine if a planet's orbit falls in the habitable zone
5
Element identification — identify 86 periodic table elements from pure emission spectra; three difficulty tiers with progressive scaffolding removal
6
Electron configuration — after correct element ID, determine ground-state electron configuration using the Aufbau principle; three scaffold tiers (full config → valence only → blank)
7
Planetary habitability — evaluate four instrument readings (atmospheric pressure 0.3–3.0 atm, surface temperature −20 to +70°C, magnetic field ≥8 μT, orbital stability ≥55) to classify a world
8
Hull Integrity — 5-segment ship health displayed in the HUD. Wrong answers remove segments; 5 correct in a row restores one. If all five are lost the current sector resets (score preserved). Teaches persistence and accuracy over speed.
9
Anomaly identification — every 8–12 stars a full-screen alert fires for a non-OBAFGKM object: Magnetar, Pulsar, Tidal Disruption Event, or Quasar. Students identify it from real physical characteristics. +200 pts.
10
First Contact sequence — five transmissions unlock as students find habitable worlds. Each is a different puzzle type: Fibonacci pattern recognition, binary image decode, classify an alien star from its spectrum, calculate light-travel time, and a final revelation. Based on Sagan's Contact framework.
11
Forbidden lines (Tier 3+) — students compare stellar vs nebular spectra and identify why certain emission lines are physically impossible in laboratory conditions. Teaches low-density plasma physics. +150 pts.
12
Advanced spectroscopy (Research Mode) — HR diagram placement, spectroscopic binary mass ratios, lithium depletion age estimation, interstellar absorption line identification, radial velocity planet detection.

Game structure

The game is organised into 10 sectors across 5 tiers. Each sector introduces new puzzle complexity.

T1
Home Nebula, Habitable Belt — absorption spectra only; full scaffolding; Doppler bonus unlocked
T2
White Ring, Red Dwarf Field — adds emission spectra; Doppler required; special events begin
T3
Inner Rim, Deep Field — binary composite spectra dominate; all advanced operations active
T4
Stellar Graveyard, Void Margin, Forbidden Zone — unlocks after all 6 base sectors visited; increased mystery object frequency
T5
The Heliopause — named halo stars; three advanced advanced operations (lithium age, radial velocity, ISM absorption); unlocks Research Mode

Each star scan follows a 4-act loop: emission match → planetary survey → stellar classification → civilisation outcome. Acts II–IV only appear when relevant (e.g. Act II only if the star has a habitable candidate).

Progression & unlocks

Difficulty increases automatically as students play — no teacher configuration required.

→
0–19 stars: Element ID Tier A (full emission strip shown, choose from 4)
→
20+ stars: Element ID becomes mandatory; Tier B active (strips visible for first 5 correct, then name-only)
→
Every 8–12 stars: Anomaly puzzle fires — full-screen alert, student must identify the exotic object type
→
Electron configuration follows every correct element ID — 3 scaffold tiers, progressive difficulty
→
Colony 3: Deep Field Telescope unlocked (50 credits/scan; 19 curated real deep-field targets)
→
Habitable worlds found: First Contact signal advances — 5 transmissions unlock progressively
→
Research Mode: unlocks after first Heliopause discovery; HR diagram replaces OBAFGKM buttons

First-scan tutorial

Players who select "New to This" and close the guide in under 20 seconds without reading it will automatically receive a 3-step interactive tutorial on their first scan. The tutorial points at the target spectrum and candidate strips with highlighted outlines and plain-language prompts — it does not give the answer. It fires only once and only for new players. Intermediate and Advanced players never see it.

Difficulty tiers — stellar classification (Act III)

Students choose a difficulty level at the start. This affects what information is shown during the stellar classification puzzle:

🌱
Cadet (Beginner) — shows the exact temperature range (e.g. "3,700–5,200 K") alongside luminosity, radius, and spectrum. Students can look the range up directly on the reference card. Recommended for first exposure to OBAFGKM.
🔭
Commander (Intermediate) — shows a qualitative thermal signature (e.g. "cool" or "very hot") plus luminosity and spectrum. Students must reason from relative temperature to class — not a lookup. Recommended for students who have covered basic HR diagram concepts.
⭐
Admiral (Advanced) — no temperature hint. Luminosity, radius, and spectrum description only. Students must know the relationships. Tier 4/5 sectors additionally replace values with the Stefan-Boltzmann formula (L = 4πR²σT⁴) for students to solve.

Teachers can advise students which level to select based on prior curriculum coverage. Difficulty affects Act III only — all other puzzles scale by sector tier.

Class Mode — focused activity URLs

Add a URL parameter to focus the game on a specific puzzle type for in-class activities. Students see no difference in the interface — the parameter works silently.

?mode=classify — OBAFGKM stellar classification (Act III)
?mode=spectra — emission spectrum matching (Act I)
?mode=survey — planetary habitability survey (Act II)
?mode=element — element identification puzzles
?mode=kepler — Kepler's Law orbital distance puzzles
?mode=free — full game, no restriction (default)

Combine with difficulty selection: share a URL and tell students which difficulty to pick when they land on the name screen.

Session reports & assessment

After transmitting a discovery, students see a Mission Report with accuracy per puzzle category, total score, stars found, time played, and Kepler working shown (T, T², T²×M, calculated vs student a, % error). The report can be:

→
Copied as plain text (includes version number) for submission via email, LMS, or Google Form
→
Printed directly using the Print Session Card button — formatted as a single-page lab report

Research Mode upgrades the report to a full Research Log — formatted as an observational astronomy report suitable for introductory lab submission.

Glossary & reference tools

The in-game glossary (📖 button) contains four tabs:

📖
Terms — 76 alphabetised astronomy and chemistry definitions; fully translated into Spanish
🌈
Element Spectra — rendered emission strips for all 86 game-pool elements; lazy-loaded
⚛️
Configurations — ground-state electron configurations for all 86 elements, grouped by s/p/d/f block; searchable
🔬
Periodic Table — all 118 elements; gold-bordered elements are in the game pool; click any gold element for a step-by-step Aufbau walkthrough with plain-language bus-seat analogy; guided tour suggests H → B → Ne → Sc → La to demonstrate all four blocks

Bilingual support

The game is fully playable in English and Spanish. Language is selected on the title screen and can be changed at any time. The following are translated: all UI strings and act headings, the full How to Play guide, all 5 guide sections (Reading Spectra, Star Classes, Planet Survey, Advanced Operations, Research Mode), and all 65+ Spanish glossary terms. The Periodic Table, Element Spectra, and Configuration tabs function identically in both languages.

Accessibility & motion

✓ Photosensitivity notice on title screen with Reduce Motion toggle
✓ Reduce Motion disables hyperspace bars, mystery object flicker, and score burst — all gameplay intact
✓ Font size toggle (A button in HUD) — three sizes
✓ Zoom control (+ / − in HUD) — 80% to 140%; auto-scales to screen resolution (1080p=100%, 1440p=120%, 4K=140%)
✓ Full bilingual UI and guide content (English / Spanish)
✓ All puzzles completable without audio — music and chimes are supplementary
⚠ Colour-dependent content: Spectral identification currently relies on colour perception. Students with colour vision deficiency can still use line positions (wavelength values shown in nm) and pattern spacing — real spectroscopists use wavelength numbers rather than colour. A dedicated Wavelength Mode (high-contrast monochrome + nm labels on all lines) is planned for a future release.

Privacy & data

✓ No accounts required — students choose any display name or callsign
✓ No personal data collected or transmitted
✓ Leaderboard stores only chosen display name and score
✓ All session data stays in the browser — nothing logged externally
✓ Runs entirely as a single HTML file — no installation, no server required
✓ Works offline after first load

Content guidance

Suitable for ages 14+. Contains: real historical science facts (including Hypatia of Alexandria); references to extreme astrophysical phenomena in scientific context only; a global leaderboard using chosen callsigns not real names. No violent, sexual, or inappropriate content. Colonisation narrative involves peaceful science settlements. Element assignments in identification puzzles are educational and do not claim to represent actual spectral compositions of named stars.

Curriculum alignment

Suitable for high school astronomy, physics, and chemistry (grades 9–12) and introductory collegiate astronomy.

→
NGSS: ESS1-A, ESS1-B, PS4-B (electromagnetic radiation and stellar properties)
→
AP Physics / AP Environmental Science: stellar classification, atomic emission, Kepler's Laws, orbital mechanics
→
IB Physics and Chemistry: spectroscopy SL/HL, atomic structure, electron configuration, Kepler's Laws
→
College intro astronomy: OBAFGKM sequence, HR diagram, habitable zones, stellar evolution, spectroscopic binaries
→
Research Mode (Heliopause): aligns with Carroll & Ostlie Ch. 7 (binary mass), observational astronomy lab formats

⬡ Stellar Arcade
Anomaly Response
Navigate your ship on an isometric 30×30 sector grid.

⚡ Token Phase — two simultaneous 20s challenges:
  Reactor Charge — press Space when bar hits green zone → move tokens
  Circuit Repair — click numbered nodes in order → laser tokens
  Zone shrinks & bar accelerates with each token earned

📋 Plan — assign moves and lasers across 4 phases:
  ↑ forward  ·  ← turn & move left  ·  → turn & move right
  ⟨⚡ laser left  ·  ⟩⚡ laser right   (range: 3 tiles)

〜 Nebula — drift one extra tile after landing
⊕ Black Hole — teleports your ship around the vortex
◆ Asteroid — collision deals 1 hull damage

Ships have 5 Hull Points. First turn grants 4 move + 4 laser tokens free.

Mini-Game · Spectra Explorers

Spectra Rush

Spectra bars fall from space. Identify the star type before it hits the bottom.

Two round types alternate:

LABEL MODE — type name is shown, match fast VISUAL MODE — no label, pick the matching reference

Bars enter from the left (blue side) or right (red side).
At higher rounds, the spectrum itself flips too.

Keys 1–7 = O B A F G K M in label mode

Mini-Game · Spectra Explorers

Wavelength Catcher

Elements fall as glowing blocks.
Each block shows a coloured emission dot.

Move the paddle so the dot lands on its
matching colour on the spectrum bar below.

The bar is your answer key — always visible.

SINGLE — one dot per block DUAL — catch the ringed dot STORM — two blocks at once
Mouse or touch · Click canvas to lock cursor · ESC to release

⚠ STRUCTURAL FAILURE IMMINENT

Hull Breach

Patch the hull breaches before structural integrity fails.

Select a patch from the kit, then place it anywhere on the breach grid.
Seal the entire breach — use fewer pieces for higher efficiency scores.
A perfect fit with minimum pieces earns a ✦ MASTERPIECE.
Neglected breaches grow larger at higher levels.

R rotate CCW  ·  E rotate CW  ·  F flip  ·  Z undo  ·  ESC deselect

· Seal the breaches · Save the ship ·
